title: Pie and mash with liquor
subtitle: Minced beef pie with buttery mash and a bright green parsley sauce
excerpt: Pie and mash is a London working-class institution, still served in the same tiled shops that have been there for a century. The minced beef pie, buttery mash, and sharp green parsley liquor are a combination unlike anything else in British food.
sourceNotes: London pie-and-mash tradition dating from the mid-19th century. Originally served with eels, the pie and mash shop became a fixture of East and South London from the Victorian era. The liquor was originally made from the water used to boil eels, but modern versions use vegetable stock with parsley. Classic London working-class food.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the pies before baking for up to 2 months. The mash and liquor are best made fresh.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pie filling and pastry can be assembled the night before and chilled, then baked on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: In the pie-and-mash tradition, liquor is a thin bright-green parsley sauce made from stock, cornflour, and a large quantity of fresh parsley. It is not a gravy and not an alcohol-based sauce. It is poured generously over the pie and mash.

content.text: is one of the most specific dishes in British regional cooking. The pies are small, with a hot water crust or shortcrust base, filled with a simple minced beef and onion mixture. The mash is plain and buttery. The liquor is thin, bright green, and poured over everything at the table. It is sharp with parsley and unlike any other sauce in the British kitchen.

items.prepNote: floury variety, peeled and cut into chunks
finely chopped, leaves and fine stalks

content.text: Make the filling and bake the pies

Heat a frying pan over a medium-high heat. Cook the onion in a little oil for 6 minutes until soft. Add the mince and brown, breaking it up. Add the stock and Worcestershire sauce. Season and simmer for 10 minutes until the liquid reduces to a thick, clingy gravy. Cool.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line four individual small tins or one rectangular tin with two-thirds of the pastry. Fill with the cooled mince mixture. Top with the remaining pastry, press edges to seal, and brush with egg. Make a small steam hole in each pie top.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the pastry is golden and crisp. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.
Boil the potatoes in salted water for 18 to 20 minutes until tender. Drain well and mash with the butter and warm milk until smooth. Season generously.

Bring the vegetable stock to a simmer in a medium saucepan. Whisk in the cornflour mixture and stir for 2 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly. It should be thin rather than thick. Stir in the parsley off the heat and season.
Serve a pie on each plate alongside a generous mound of mash. Pour the liquor over both generously at the table.

items.cause: Too much cornflour or reduced too long.
items.fix: Liquor should pour freely. Add a splash more stock and stir to loosen.

items.symptom: The liquor loses its bright green colour
items.cause: Parsley added while the sauce was too hot and cooked in.
items.fix: Add the parsley off the heat just before serving.

items.cause: Filling was too wet when assembled.
items.fix: Reduce the filling until the liquid is almost fully absorbed before cooling and adding to the pastry.

items.cause: Overworked or blended rather than mashed.
items.fix: Use a potato ricer or hand masher. Never blend or food-process potatoes for mash.

content.text: Pie-and-mash shops have operated in East and South London since the Victorian era, when they fed the city's working poor with cheap, filling food. The shops are distinguished by white tiles, wooden benches, and marble counters, and the menu has barely changed in a hundred years. The liquor was originally made from the water in which live eels were boiled, but modern shops use vegetable stock. The dish remains a living piece of London working-class food history.
